---
title: "Dataset_Cleaning"
output: html_document
date: "2026-03-27"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

```{r dataset cleaning}
############################################################
# Prepare dataset for GDP growth comparison + choropleth map
#
# PURPOSE OF THIS SCRIPT:
# This script takes the raw dataset, cleans it, reshapes it,
# checks for duplicate and invalid rows, creates ISO3 country codes,
# gives long indicator names shorter variable names,
# removes GDP per capita growth outliers,
# drops very incomplete columns,
# and saves the final cleaned file as Dataset_Cleaned.csv
############################################################

# ---- Packages ----
packages <- c(
  "tidyverse", "janitor", "countrycode"
)

to_install <- packages[!packages %in% installed.packages()[, "Package"]]
if (length(to_install) > 0) install.packages(to_install)

library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)
library(countrycode)

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# 0.5) DATASET DESCRIPTION (BEFORE cleaning)
# This function prints a simple overview of the raw dataset:
# - file name
# - number of rows and columns
# - column names
# - data types
# - missing values
# - unique countries
# - unique indicators
# ----------------------------------------------------------
describe_dataset <- function(df, name = "dataset") {
  cat("\n================ DATASET DESCRIPTION ================\n")
  cat("Dataset name:", name, "\n")
  cat("Rows:", nrow(df), " | Columns:", ncol(df), "\n\n")
  
  cat("Column names:\n")
  print(names(df))
  
  cat("\nColumn types:\n")
  print(sapply(df, class))
  
  miss <- colSums(is.na(df))
  cat("\nMissing values by column (NA count):\n")
  print(miss)
  
  cat("\nTotal missing values in entire dataset (all NAs):\n")
  print(sum(is.na(df)))
  
  # If the dataset contains a Country Name column,
  # print all unique countries found in the raw data
  if ("Country Name" %in% names(df)) {
    countries_unique <- df %>%
      distinct(`Country Name`) %>%
      mutate(`Country Name` = str_squish(`Country Name`)) %>%
      arrange(`Country Name`)
    
    cat("\nNumber of unique countries:", nrow(countries_unique), "\n")
    cat("\n================ UNIQUE COUNTRIES (RAW) ================\n")
    print(countries_unique)
    cat("========================================================\n")
  }
  
  # If the dataset contains a Series Name column,
  # print all unique indicators found in the raw data
  if ("Series Name" %in% names(df)) {
    cat("\nNumber of unique indicators:", length(unique(df$`Series Name`)), "\n")
    cat("\n================ UNIQUE INDICATORS (RAW) ================\n")
    print(sort(unique(df$`Series Name`)))
    cat("========================================================\n")
  }
  
  cat("=====================================================\n\n")
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# 1) Load raw dataset
# The file should be saved in the same folder as this script
# check.names = FALSE keeps original column names unchanged
# ----------------------------------------------------------
cat("\n[STEP 1] Loading raw dataset...\n")

data_raw <- read.csv("dataset.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE, check.names = FALSE)

# Print a description of the raw dataset before cleaning
describe_dataset(data_raw, name = "Raw dataset.csv")

# glimpse() gives a quick preview of the structure
glimpse(data_raw)

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# 2) Basic Cleaning
# Goal:
# - remove bad rows such as file notes / update rows first
# - standardise column names
# - remove unnecessary metadata columns
# - detect year columns
# - reshape data from wide format to long format
# ----------------------------------------------------------
cat("\n[STEP 2] Starting basic cleaning...\n")

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# Remove invalid / metadata rows FIRST using original column names
# These are not real country observations.
# For example:
# "Data from database: ..."
# "Last Updated: ..."
# ----------------------------------------------------------
data_step0 <- data_raw %>%
  mutate(
    `Country Name` = str_squish(`Country Name`),
    `Country Code` = str_squish(`Country Code`),
    `Series Name`  = str_squish(`Series Name`)
  ) %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(`Country Name`), `Country Name` != "",
    !is.na(`Series Name`), `Series Name` != "",
    !str_detect(`Country Name`, "^Data from database:"),
    !str_detect(`Country Name`, "^Last Updated:")
  )

cat("\nRows after removing bad metadata rows:\n")
cat("Rows:", nrow(data_step0), "\n")

# clean_names() converts column names into lower-case snake_case
# Example: "Country Name" -> "country_name"
data_step1 <- data_step0 %>%
  clean_names()

cat("\nColumn names after clean_names():\n")
print(names(data_step1))

# Remove series_code if it exists
# any_of() avoids errors if the column is not present
data_step2 <- data_step1 %>%
  select(-any_of(c("series_code")))

# Make sure important columns exist before moving on
stopifnot(all(c("country_name", "country_code", "series_name") %in% colnames(data_step2)))

# Trim whitespace again after renaming to keep later steps safe
data_step2 <- data_step2 %>%
  mutate(
    country_name = str_squish(country_name),
    country_code = str_squish(country_code),
    series_name  = str_squish(series_name)
  )

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# Detect year columns automatically
# This handles names like:
# 2020
# X2020
# 2020_yr2020
# X2020_yr2020
# ----------------------------------------------------------
year_cols <- colnames(data_step2)[
  str_detect(colnames(data_step2), "^x?\\d{4}_yr\\d{4}$|^x?\\d{4}$")
]

cat("\nDetected year columns:\n")
print(year_cols)

if (length(year_cols) == 0) stop("No year columns found.")

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# Convert from wide format to long format
# Before:
# one row per country-indicator with years across columns
#
# After:
# one row per country-year-indicator
# ----------------------------------------------------------
panel_long <- data_step2 %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = all_of(year_cols),
    names_to = "year_raw",
    values_to = "value"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    year = str_extract(year_raw, "\\d{4}") %>% as.integer(),
    value = as.numeric(value)
  ) %>%
  select(country_name, country_code, series_name, year, value)

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# Handle duplicate rows if present
# A duplicate means the same country + year + indicator appears
# more than once.
#
# If duplicates are found, keep the first non-missing value.
# ----------------------------------------------------------
dup_check <- panel_long %>%
  count(country_name, country_code, year, series_name) %>%
  filter(n > 1)

if (nrow(dup_check) > 0) {
  cat("\nDuplicate country-year-indicator rows found:\n")
  print(dup_check)
  
  panel_long <- panel_long %>%
    group_by(country_name, country_code, year, series_name) %>%
    summarise(value = first(na.omit(value)), .groups = "drop")
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# Convert back from long format to wide format
# Final result:
# one row per country-year
# each indicator becomes its own column
# ----------------------------------------------------------
panel_wide <- panel_long %>%
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols = c(country_name, country_code, year),
    names_from = series_name,
    values_from = value
  ) %>%
  clean_names()

panel_model <- panel_wide

cat("\nLoaded dataset columns:\n")
print(colnames(panel_model))

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# 3) List all unique countries in cleaned panel dataset
# This helps verify which countries remain after cleaning
# ----------------------------------------------------------
cat("\n[STEP 3] Listing all unique countries...\n")

unique_countries <- panel_model %>%
  distinct(country_name) %>%
  arrange(country_name)

cat("\n================ UNIQUE COUNTRIES ================\n")
print(unique_countries)
cat("Total unique countries:", nrow(unique_countries), "\n")
cat("==================================================\n\n")

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# 4) Add ISO3 country codes
# ISO3 is the standard 3-letter code used in maps and analysis
# Example:
# Ireland = IRL
# Japan   = JPN
#
# If country_code is already valid, use it.
# Otherwise, try converting from country_name.
# ----------------------------------------------------------
cat("\n[STEP 4] Creating ISO3 country codes...\n")

panel_model <- panel_model %>%
  mutate(country_name = str_squish(country_name))

panel_model <- panel_model %>%
  mutate(
    iso3c = if_else(
      str_detect(country_code, "^[A-Z]{3}$"),
      country_code,
      countrycode(country_name, origin = "country.name", destination = "iso3c")
    )
  )

# Show unmatched countries, if any
unmatched <- panel_model %>%
  filter(is.na(iso3c)) %>%
  distinct(country_name)

if (nrow(unmatched) > 0) {
  cat("\nCountries not matched to ISO3:\n")
  print(unmatched)
} else {
  cat("\nAll countries matched to ISO3 successfully.\n")
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# 5) Rename indicator columns to shorter and clearer names
#
# Why this step matters:
# Raw indicator names from sources like IMF / World Bank are often
# very long and hard to use in analysis.
#
# This function:
# - keeps key ID columns as simple names
# - gives known variables standard short names
# - converts all other columns into safe snake_case names
# ----------------------------------------------------------
cat("\n[STEP 5] Rename indicator columns to shorter and clearer names...\n")

rename_to_short <- function(df) {
  old_names <- names(df)
  new_names <- old_names
  
  # Keep identifying columns simple and consistent
  new_names <- ifelse(old_names == "country_name", "country", new_names)
  new_names <- ifelse(old_names == "country_code", "country_code", new_names)
  new_names <- ifelse(old_names == "year", "year", new_names)
  new_names <- ifelse(old_names == "iso3c", "iso3c", new_names)
  
  # Rename common economics variables to shorter names
  # Only rename when the matching column actually exists
  new_names <- ifelse(
    str_detect(old_names, "^gdp_growth_annual_percent$|^gdp_growth$|^real_gdp_growth$"),
    "gdp_growth",
    new_names
  )
  
  new_names <- ifelse(
    str_detect(old_names, "gdp_per_capita_growth"),
    "gdp_pc_growth",
    new_names
  )
  
  new_names <- ifelse(
    str_detect(old_names, "inflation_consumer_prices"),
    "inflation",
    new_names
  )
  
  new_names <- ifelse(
    str_detect(old_names, "gross_capital_formation"),
    "gross_capital_formation_pct_gdp",
    new_names
  )
  
  new_names <- ifelse(
    str_detect(old_names, "current_account_balance"),
    "current_account_balance_pct_gdp",
    new_names
  )
  
  new_names <- ifelse(
    str_detect(old_names, "labor_force_participation_rate"),
    "labour_force_participation",
    new_names
  )
  
  new_names <- ifelse(
    str_detect(old_names, "exports_of_goods_and_services"),
    "exports_pct_gdp",
    new_names
  )
  
  new_names <- ifelse(
    str_detect(old_names, "net_lending_net_borrowing"),
    "fiscal_balance_pct_gdp",
    new_names
  )
  
  # For all remaining columns that were not manually renamed:
  # clean them into simple snake_case variable names
  generic_idx <- new_names == old_names &
    !(old_names %in% c("country_name", "country_code", "year", "iso3c"))
  
  new_names[generic_idx] <- old_names[generic_idx] %>%
    str_replace_all("&", " and ") %>%
    str_replace_all("\\+", " plus ") %>%
    str_replace_all("%", " percent ") %>%
    str_replace_all("/", " ") %>%
    str_replace_all("\\(", " ") %>%
    str_replace_all("\\)", " ") %>%
    str_replace_all(",", " ") %>%
    str_replace_all("-", " ") %>%
    str_squish() %>%
    str_to_lower() %>%
    str_replace_all("[^a-z0-9]+", "_") %>%
    str_replace_all("^_|_$", "")
  
  # make.unique() prevents duplicate column names
  new_names <- make.unique(new_names, sep = "_")
  names(df) <- new_names
  df
}

panel_short <- rename_to_short(panel_model)

cat("\nColumns after renaming:\n")
print(colnames(panel_short))

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# 6) Check GDP variables properly
# We want to make sure the dataset includes gdp_pc_growth.
# gdp_growth may or may not exist separately.
# ----------------------------------------------------------
cat("\n[STEP 6] Checking GDP-related variables...\n")

if (!("gdp_pc_growth" %in% names(panel_short))) {
  stop("No gdp_pc_growth variable found.")
}

if ("gdp_growth" %in% names(panel_short)) {
  cat("\nA separate gdp_growth variable exists in the dataset.\n")
} else {
  cat("\nNo separate gdp_growth variable exists in this dataset.\n")
  cat("Keeping only gdp_pc_growth as the GDP-related growth variable.\n")
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# 7) Remove outliers in gdp_pc_growth using the IQR rule
#
# Layman explanation:
# Outliers are unusually extreme values that are far away
# from most other observations.
#
# IQR method:
# lower bound = Q1 - 1.5*IQR
# upper bound = Q3 + 1.5*IQR
#
# Rows outside these bounds are removed only for gdp_pc_growth.
# ----------------------------------------------------------
cat("\n[STEP 7] Checking and removing outliers in gdp_pc_growth...\n")

if ("gdp_pc_growth" %in% names(panel_short)) {
  q1 <- quantile(panel_short$gdp_pc_growth, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
  q3 <- quantile(panel_short$gdp_pc_growth, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  iqr_value <- IQR(panel_short$gdp_pc_growth, na.rm = TRUE)
  
  lower_bound <- q1 - 1.5 * iqr_value
  upper_bound <- q3 + 1.5 * iqr_value
  
  rows_before <- nrow(panel_short)
  
  outliers_removed <- panel_short %>%
    filter(!is.na(gdp_pc_growth)) %>%
    filter(gdp_pc_growth < lower_bound | gdp_pc_growth > upper_bound) %>%
    select(country, year, gdp_pc_growth)
  
  panel_short <- panel_short %>%
    filter(is.na(gdp_pc_growth) | (gdp_pc_growth >= lower_bound & gdp_pc_growth <= upper_bound))
  
  rows_after <- nrow(panel_short)
  
  cat("\nOutlier removal for gdp_pc_growth completed.\n")
  cat("Lower bound:", lower_bound, "\n")
  cat("Upper bound:", upper_bound, "\n")
  cat("Rows removed:", rows_before - rows_after, "\n")
  
  if (nrow(outliers_removed) > 0) {
    cat("\nRemoved outlier rows:\n")
    print(outliers_removed)
  } else {
    cat("\nNo outliers found in gdp_pc_growth.\n")
  }
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# 8a) Remove selected columns from final output
# country_code is removed because iso3c is kept instead
# ----------------------------------------------------------
cat("\n[STEP 8a] Removing unnecessary columns...\n")

panel_short <- panel_short %>%
  select(-any_of(c("country_code")))

cat("\nRemoved from final output (if present): country_code\n")

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# 8b) Remove empty columns + high-missing columns
#
# First:
# remove columns that are completely empty
#
# Then:
# move key columns to the front for readability
#
# Finally:
# drop columns with more than 40% missing values,
# except for important core variables we want to keep
# ----------------------------------------------------------
cat("\n[STEP 8b] Removing empty and highly incomplete columns...\n")

# Remove columns where every value is NA
panel_short <- panel_short %>%
  select(where(~ !all(is.na(.))))

# Put key columns first, but only if they exist
first_cols <- c("country", "iso3c", "year", "gdp_growth", "gdp_pc_growth")
existing_first_cols <- first_cols[first_cols %in% names(panel_short)]

panel_short <- panel_short %>%
  relocate(all_of(existing_first_cols))

# Calculate missing-value share for each column
missing_share <- sapply(panel_short, function(x) mean(is.na(x)))

# Keep these columns even if they exceed 40% missing
keep_core <- c("country", "iso3c", "year", "gdp_growth", "gdp_pc_growth")

# Identify columns to drop
drop_cols <- names(missing_share[missing_share > 0.40])
drop_cols <- setdiff(drop_cols, keep_core)

# Drop highly missing columns
panel_short <- panel_short %>%
  select(-any_of(drop_cols))

cat("\nDropped columns (>40% missing):\n")
print(drop_cols)

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# 9) Final dataset overview
# This gives a quick check of the finished cleaned dataset
# ----------------------------------------------------------
cat("\n[STEP 9] Final dataset overview...\n")

cat("\nFinal cleaned dataset columns:\n")
print(colnames(panel_short))

cat("\nFinal cleaned dataset dimensions:\n")
cat("Rows:", nrow(panel_short), " | Columns:", ncol(panel_short), "\n")

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# 10) Save final cleaned dataset
# This writes the final cleaned file to the working folder
# ----------------------------------------------------------
cat("\n[STEP 10] Saving cleaned dataset...\n")

write.csv(panel_short, "Dataset_Cleaned.csv", row.names = FALSE)

cat("\nSaved Dataset_Cleaned.csv\n")
```
